skill.name: ai-video-generation
skill.description: Generate AI videos through the inference.sh CLI using models for text-to-video, image-to-video, lipsync, avatar animation, upscaling, foley audio, and media merging.
skill.summary: Generate and process AI videos with inference.sh CLI models.
skill.author: inference-sh

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command and network patterns in SKILL.md. Most command hits are Markdown fences, inline app IDs, or normal infsh examples, but the Quick Start includes a real remote install command that pipes curl output into sh. No prompt injection, credential theft, hidden code, or malicious intent was found in the reviewed file.

medium_findings.title: Remote Install Script Piped To Shell
medium_findings.description: The Quick Start command downloads a script from https://cli.inference.sh and pipes it directly to sh before login. This is a true positive for a dangerous installation pattern because a changed or intercepted remote script would execute with the user privileges of the shell. The context appears to be normal CLI installation documentation, not confirmed malware.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell command is present on line 22. Confidence is high for the unsafe pattern, while malicious intent is not confirmed because it points to the skill vendor domain.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Sends Media Requests To Network Services
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to run infsh app commands that submit prompts and media URLs to inference.sh hosted model apps. This is expected functionality for video generation, but users should treat prompts, image URLs, audio URLs, and video URLs as data sent to an external service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple documented infsh app run examples submit prompts or media URLs. The behavior is central to the skill and legitimate, but it creates privacy and network-use risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Findings Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick findings are caused by Markdown code fences, inline app IDs, and command examples. I did not find Ruby backtick execution or hidden dynamic shell evaluation in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown documentation and CLI examples. The only materially risky command pattern is handled separately as the pipe-to-shell finding.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Documentation Or User Inputs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to the inference.sh website, documentation links, or placeholder media URLs such as portrait, audio, and clip examples. No evidence found that these URLs exfiltrate local files or secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation links and example inputs. They still indicate network use, but they do not show covert destinations or credential collection.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Text Matches Only
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm findings on the description and table header lines appear to be keyword false positives. No evidence found of DES, MD5, SHA-1, or any cryptographic operation in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain frontmatter description syntax and a documentation table header. There is no cryptographic code or configuration in the reviewed file.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe To Shell Installation Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The command curl -fsSL https://cli.inference.sh | sh executes remote installer content directly. Users should prefer a verified package manager, checksum verification, or an audited install script before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous shell pipeline is explicit and does not depend on inference. The reviewed context makes it an installation safety concern rather than confirmed malware.

content.user_title: Generate AI Videos with CLI Models
content.value_statement: Producing short AI video clips across many providers requires model-specific commands and inputs. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code focused prompts for running inference.sh video apps.
actual_capabilities: Lists inference.sh model IDs for text-to-video, image-to-video, lipsync, avatar, upscaling, foley, and media merge tasks.
Provides infsh CLI commands for generating videos from prompts.
Provides examples for animating image URLs with video models.
Shows commands for talking-head and lipsync generation with image and audio URLs.
Includes examples for adding foley audio, upscaling video, and merging clips.
Links to inference.sh documentation for running apps and streaming results.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI, account login, network access, and any required provider credits.
Does not include local video generation models or offline processing.
Example media URLs are placeholders and must be replaced with accessible user assets.
Does not validate generated content rights, consent, or platform policy compliance.

use_cases.title: Create campaign video drafts
use_cases.description: Generate short visual concepts from written prompts, then compare model outputs before producing final assets.

use_cases.title: Animate product or concept images
use_cases.description: Turn image URLs into motion clips for prototypes, social posts, or presentation material.

use_cases.title: Build avatar and narration workflows
use_cases.description: Combine portrait images and audio files to test talking-head or lipsync clips through supported model apps.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a basic video
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create an infsh command for a five second video about [scene]. Keep the prompt clear and production ready.

prompt_templates.title: Animate an image
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an image-to-video command for this image URL: [image URL]. The motion should show [desired movement] and preserve the subject.

prompt_templates.title: Create a talking avatar
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a lipsync or avatar video command using image URL [portrait URL] and audio URL [audio URL]. Recommend a suitable model.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a multi-step video pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an inference.sh workflow that generates a clip, adds foley sound, upscales it, and merges it with [other clips]. Include model choices and commands.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a short cinematic video of a forest flyover.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends a Veo text-to-video model, writes a concise scene prompt, and provides an infsh command to run the job.

output_examples.input: A designer provides a portrait image URL and narration audio URL.
output_examples.output: The assistant selects an avatar or lipsync model and describes the required image and audio inputs for a talking-head generation run.

output_examples.input: A creator has two clip URLs and wants a polished social media asset.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines a workflow for foley sound, upscaling, and clip merging with the appropriate inference.sh app categories.

best_practices: Use non-sensitive prompts and media URLs because generation requests are sent to external model services.
Verify the inference.sh CLI installer source and prefer documented installation methods before running shell installers.
Start with short clips and lower-cost models before using premium models or longer durations.
anti_patterns: Do not submit private images, unreleased product footage, or confidential audio without approval.
Do not run the pipe-to-shell installer without reviewing the source and install method.
Do not assume generated videos are cleared for commercial use without checking model and asset licenses.

faq.question: Does this skill generate video locally?
faq.answer: No. It uses the inference.sh CLI to run hosted model apps over the network.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it make videos from images?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes image-to-video examples that use accessible image URLs as inputs.

faq.question: Can it create talking-head videos?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents avatar and lipsync models that use portrait image URLs and audio URLs.

faq.question: What should users review before installation?
faq.answer: Users should review the CLI installer and avoid running remote scripts directly without verification.

faq.question: Are there privacy risks?
faq.answer: Yes. Prompts and media URLs may be sent to external services, so users should avoid sensitive content.
